nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving and still streams, flooded meadows, and backwaters with dense aquatic vegetation and fine sandy or silty substrate; often found in the middle and upper water column among vegetation rather than on the substrate
nativeRange.biotope: Western Amazon and Paraguay River basin

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate to protect delicate barbels
broad-leaved plants (Anubias, Amazon sword) as resting surfaces — fish perch on leaves rather than substrate
dense planting for cover and security
driftwood and leaf litter for tannins and biofilm
low to moderate flow — avoid strong currents

diet.notes: Micro-omnivore requiring small-sized foods suited to its tiny mouth. Accepts high-quality micro pellets, crushed flake, and sinking wafers. Live and frozen foods are strongly recommended: baby brine shrimp, daphnia, cyclops, micro bloodworms, and micro worms. Unlike most bottom-dwelling corydoras, G. hastatus is partially adapted for pelagic zooplankton foraging and benefits from mid-water feeding; food must reach the middle of the water column, not just the substrate. Feed small amounts 2–3 times daily.

goodWith: small nano tetras (ember tetra, neon tetra, green neon tetra)
dwarf shrimp (with adult shrimp — fry may be at risk)
avoidWith: large or predatory fish (any fish over 5–6 cm that could swallow them)
fast, competitive feeders that outcompete them for food

imageCandidates.notes: Standard FishBase species image; verify availability. Corydoras hastatus species photo.

summary: The Hastatus Pygmy Corydoras is the smallest corydoras species at ~2.5 cm, notable for its mid-water shoaling behaviour — it rests on plant leaves and schools in open water rather than on the substrate, resembling a tiny tetra more than a typical bottom-dweller. Easy care; requires groups of 10+.
careNotes:
Gastrodermus hastatus (formerly Corydoras hastatus until the 2024 Dias, Tencatt, Roxo et al. phylogenomic revision of the Corydoradinae, which resurrected the genus Gastrodermus Cope, 1878 and transferred hastatus into it) is the smallest member of the armoured catfish subfamily, reaching only 2.5–3.5 cm. It is a merge candidate with fw-fish-130 (“Tail Spot Cory,” listed under the pre-2024 name Corydoras hastatus): both entries describe the same species; end-of-slice cleanup should consolidate them under this entry (fw-fish-372) with the current valid name Gastrodermus hastatus.

This species defies the typical corydoras stereotype: rather than hugging the bottom, it spends most of its time in the mid-water column, hovering among plant stems and perching on broad leaves. This midwater shoaling behaviour makes it look more like a tiny characid than a catfish, and it forms mimetic associations with small tetras in the wild. Groups of fewer than 10 often school poorly, behave erratically, and show stress-related health issues; 12–15 individuals is the recommended minimum for confident schooling.

Tank setup should include a fine sand substrate (to protect barbels during occasional bottom foraging), dense planting, driftwood, and broad-leaved epiphytes like Anubias that provide resting platforms. Low to moderate flow is preferred. Water should be soft to moderately hard and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–15). A temperature range of 20–26°C suits the species well; the Aquarium Glaser source notes that a seasonal temperature cycle (cool 16–22°C / warm 24–28°C) mirrors the subtropical Paraguayan climate and may improve conditioning for breeding.

Feeding must account for the mid-water lifestyle: foods that only sink to the substrate may be missed. Offer baby brine shrimp, daphnia, and cyclops mid-column in addition to micro sinking pellets. Live or frozen micro foods are highly beneficial as the species is partially adapted to forage on pelagic zooplankton.

Like all callichthyid catfishes, G. hastatus possesses sharp, venomous pectoral and dorsal fin spines. The venom is mild but can cause painful stings when handling — use a cup or soft net rather than bare hands. The fish are also copper-sensitive (as all scaleless/lightly-scaled catfishes); avoid copper-based medications.

See also: fw-fish-130 (Tail Spot Cory / Corydoras hastatus, pre-2024 name) — MERGE CANDIDATE, same species.
breedingNotes: Moderate difficulty. G. hastatus spawns in the typical corydoras T-position. Condition a group (ideally 2–3 males per female) with live and frozen foods. Trigger spawning with a large (50–70%) water change using cooler, softer water to simulate seasonal rains. Eggs (typically 30–50) are deposited on glass, broad plant leaves, or fine-leaved plants. Parents do not guard eggs and will eat them — remove eggs or adults after spawning. Eggs hatch in 3–5 days at 24–26°C. Fry are tiny and require infusoria, paramecium, or commercial first-foods for the first week before graduating to micro worms, vinegar eels, and newly-hatched brine shrimp nauplii. Growth is slow; 3–4 months to resemble miniature adults.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras hastatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided water parameters (20–26°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 36–215 ppm), adult size (25–32 mm), minimum tank dimensions (60×30 cm), diet notes including pelagic zooplankton adaptation, midwater behaviour, and breeding trigger protocol. Note: listed under former name Corydoras hastatus; current valid name is Gastrodermus hastatus per Dias et al. 2024.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pygmy Corydoras Care Guide
additional.notes: Confirmed minimum tank 10 gal (38 L), temperature 72–78°F (22–26°C), pH 6.4–7.5, soft water; diet (frozen daphnia, cyclops, baby brine shrimp, nano pellets); midwater hovering behaviour; compatible tank mates including small tetras and rasboras.

additional.name: AquariumStoreDepot — Hastatus Cory Care Guide
additional.notes: Provided hardness (2–15 dGH), KH (1–10 dKH), lifespan (3–4 years), adult size (~2.5 cm), minimum group size 10+, native range (Amazon and Paraguay river basins, Mato Grosso, Brazil), breeding details (30–50 eggs, 3–5 day hatch), fry feeding protocol.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Gastrodermus hastatus (formerly Corydoras hastatus)
additional.notes: Confirmed new genus name Gastrodermus hastatus post-2024 revision. Noted distribution across Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Paraguay, Uruguay; adult size 2–3 cm; seasonal temperature cycling recommendation (cool 16–22°C / warm 24–28°C) for conditioning. Specimens sourced from Paraguay.

additional.name: Corydoras World — Gastrodermus lineage
additional.notes: Confirmed placement of G. hastatus within Gastrodermus (14 named species); genus established following Dias et al. 2024 phylogenomic revision resurrecting Gastrodermus Cope, 1878 from synonymy with Corydoras.

fish.breedingNotes: T-position egg depositor; 30–50 eggs per spawn on glass or plant leaves. Trigger with large cool water change. Eggs hatch in 3–5 days; fry require infusoria then micro worms and BBS nauplii. Parents eat eggs — remove after spawning.
